answers.explanation: Debits and credits are the two sides of an accounting entry. In double-entry bookkeeping, the total debits must equal the total credits.
The core rule of double-entry bookkeeping is that total debits must equal total credits, which keeps the accounts balanced.
The P&L shows income, costs and profit over a period of time, such as a month, quarter or year.
A margin shows profit as a percentage of revenue, making it easier to compare performance.
The balance sheet shows what a company owns, what it owes, and what belongs to its owners at a specific point in time.
The P&L shows income and costs by accounting period, while the cash flow statement shows actual cash movements.
Solvency is about long-term financial health, while liquidity is about whether the business has enough cash to meet short-term obligations.
The accruals principle says income and costs should be recognised in the period they relate to, not just when cash moves.
Receiving legal work in June but getting the invoice in July is an accrual: the cost is incurred before it is invoiced.
Paying annual insurance upfront and spreading the cost over 12 months is a prepayment: the cost is paid before the benefit is received.
Bookkeeping records transactions, while accounting interprets, adjusts and reports the numbers.
Month end is the recurring process of finalising the accounts for a month.
Year end is the process of closing the accounts for the financial year, which is bigger and more formal than month end.
A journal is an accounting entry posted directly into the ledger to record adjustments and corrections.
Depreciation spreads the cost of a fixed asset over the period the business expects to use it.
An invoice is a document requesting payment for goods or services.
An audit is an independent review of a company's financial statements and supporting records.
Management accounts are internal reports used for understanding performance and making decisions.
Statutory accounts are official financial statements prepared to meet legal and regulatory requirements.
Management accounts help run the business, while statutory accounts meet formal, external reporting requirements.
EBITDA stands for Earnings Before Interest, Tax, Depreciation and Amortisation.
Variable costs and cost of sales are costs that usually increase when the business sells more.
FP&A focuses on budgeting, forecasting, analysis and planning to help leadership make decisions.
A company is usually a separate legal entity owned by shareholders, unlike a partnership.
A branch is an extension of the same legal entity, while a subsidiary is a separate legal entity owned by the parent.
An accounting qualification shows someone has formal training and assessment in accounting or finance.
Tax is money paid to government under law, used to fund public services.
VAT, GST and sales tax are taxes charged on sales of goods or services and collected from customers.
PAYE and PAYG are systems where employers deduct employment tax through payroll and pay it to the tax authority.
Gearing measures how much a business relies on debt compared with equity.
Financial controls are processes and checks used to protect the accuracy and reliability of financial information.
The Chart of Accounts is the structured list of accounts used to categorise financial transactions.
Reporting dimensions are labels added to transactions so finance teams can analyse performance by department, location, project or similar categories.
Consolidated reporting combines multiple entities' financial results into one group-level view.
Eliminations are adjustments that remove transactions and balances between entities in the same group.
Mayday helps finance teams automate and control recurring month-end workflows.
Deferred Revenue and Prepayments both help spread income or costs into the correct accounting periods.
Flux helps finance teams compare month-on-month P&Ls and add variance commentary.
Recharger, Balancer and BRAG all help with multi-entity and intercompany month-end workflows.
Mayday HQ helps manage group-wide accounting structures, while Close Management helps organise tasks, reviews and close progress.
